summary: In his 2009 Annual Report letter, Warren Buffett outlines Berkshire Hathaway's core operating principles, emphasizing the importance of measuring performance against a fixed benchmark, maintaining fortress-like liquidity, and decentralizing management. He details the economic models of Berkshire's key sectors—insurance (float), regulated utilities (the social compact), and manufacturing—while candidly discussing both his successes and his mistakes.

principles.statement: Measure performance against a rational and unbending standard.
principles.rationale: Having a fixed benchmark prevents the temptation of retroactively justifying performance by moving the goalposts.
principles.source_quote: That keeps us from the temptation of seeing where the arrow of performance lands and then painting the bull’s eye around it.

principles.statement: Stick to businesses whose profit picture for decades to come seems reasonably predictable.
principles.rationale: Even if an industry is guaranteed to experience dramatic growth, competitive dynamics can destroy profit margins and returns on capital for the companies within it.
principles.source_quote: Just because Charlie and I can clearly see dramatic growth ahead for an industry does not mean we can judge what its profit margins and returns on capital will be as a host of competitors battle for supremacy.

principles.statement: Maintain massive liquidity and never become dependent on the kindness of strangers.
principles.rationale: Financial independence ensures survival during systemic panics and allows the company to act as a supplier of liquidity when others are desperate.
principles.source_quote: We will always arrange our affairs so that any requirements for cash we may conceivably have will be dwarfed by our own liquidity.

principles.statement: Grant managers extreme autonomy and avoid stifling bureaucracy.
principles.rationale: The invisible costs of slow decision-making in a bureaucracy are far worse than the visible costs of a few bad decisions made by independent managers.
principles.source_quote: We would rather suffer the visible costs of a few bad decisions than incur the many invisible costs that come from decisions made too slowly – or not at all – because of a stifling bureaucracy.

principles.statement: Do not attempt to woo Wall Street or short-term investors.
principles.rationale: A company should seek shareholders who understand the business and share its long-term goals, creating a compatible partnership rather than a transient investor base.
principles.source_quote: Investors who buy and sell based upon media or analyst commentary are not for us. Instead we want partners who join us at Berkshire because they wish to make a long-term investment in a business they themselves understand

frameworks.name: Inversion
frameworks.when_to_apply: When trying to solve difficult problems, evaluate risk, or plan for the future.
steps: Identify the ultimate goal or desired outcome.
Determine the exact actions, scenarios, or conditions that would guarantee failure or disaster.
frameworks.source_quote: “All I want to know is where I’m going to die, so I’ll never go there.” That bit of wisdom was inspired by Jacobi, the great Prussian mathematician, who counseled “Invert, always invert” as an aid to solving difficult problems.

mental_models.name: Insurance Float
mental_models.description: A 'collect-now, pay-later' model where an insurer holds large sums of money that will eventually be paid out in claims. In the interim, the company can invest this money for its own benefit. If underwriting is profitable, this float acts as cost-free leverage.
mental_models.example: Berkshire's float grew to $62 billion in 2009. Because they operated at an underwriting profit, holding this $62 billion was effectively cost-free, acting like a massive, interest-free deposit they could invest.

mental_models.name: The Social Compact
mental_models.description: A symbiotic relationship in capital-intensive, essential industries (like utilities and railroads) where the company commits to massive, far-sighted investments to serve the public, and in return, regulators allow an appropriate return on that capital.
mental_models.example: MidAmerican Energy reinvested all its earnings over a decade, including $3 billion on wind generation, fulfilling its end of the compact. In return, regulators allowed them to earn a fair return on those investments.

heuristics: Too-big-to-fail is not a fallback position.
Never become dependent on the kindness of strangers.

signature_quotes.text: That keeps us from the temptation of seeing where the arrow of performance lands and then painting the bull’s eye around it.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing why Berkshire uses the S&P 500 as a fixed, unbending standard for measuring performance.

signature_quotes.text: We will never become dependent on the kindness of strangers. Too-big-to-fail is not a fallback position at Berkshire.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining Berkshire's strict policy on maintaining massive liquidity.

signature_quotes.text: We would rather suffer the visible costs of a few bad decisions than incur the many invisible costs that come from decisions made too slowly – or not at all – because of a stifling bureaucracy.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the rationale behind Berkshire's highly decentralized management structure.

signature_quotes.text: If Charlie, I and Ajit are ever in a sinking boat – and you can only save one of us – swim to Ajit.
signature_quotes.context: Praising Ajit Jain's immense value to Berkshire Hathaway's reinsurance business.

anti_patterns.description: Investing in exciting, rapidly growing industries without predictable competitive dynamics.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Dramatic industry growth attracts a host of competitors battling for supremacy, which decimates profit margins and returns on capital, leaving even the survivors bleeding.

anti_patterns.description: Micromanaging subsidiaries with committees, budget presentations, and multiple layers of management.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It creates a stifling bureaucracy that causes decisions to be made too slowly or not at all, incurring massive invisible costs and destroying the owner-oriented attitude of managers.

anti_patterns.description: Using year-to-year stock market prices as the primary measure of a company's progress.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Market prices can be extraordinarily erratic and greatly distorted by foolishly high or low prices at the beginning or end of the measurement period.
